claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 13 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to ask you some questions about your child. 

**First, how old is your child?** 

The eligibility rules differ based on age:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over typically don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - Alex, who is 13 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 13 years old.

Since Alex is under 16, let me move to the next check in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

This check applies to ensure that children aren't working full-time. Since Alex is only 13, they're likely not working full-time, but I need to confirm this.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all. They're only 13, so they're just at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I expected! Alex is at school and not working, so that's fine.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

In other words, does Alex receive benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in their own name? 

Since Alex is 13 and at school, I'm guessing they don't receive these benefits, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name. They're just a normal schoolkid.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's completely normal for a 13-year-old at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Next question:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

This is relevant because certain types of apprenticeships can affect eligibility (though the rules vary by region). Since Alex is 13 and at school, I'm expecting the answer is no, but I need to check.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't doing any apprenticeship. They're too young for that - just doing regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's right! Alex is definitely too young for an apprenticeship at 13.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to ask about your residency status, as this is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (for up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?** Most people will simply normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK. I live here with Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You meet the residency requirement as you normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to confirm that you're responsible for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You qualify if ANY of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex** (this is the most common situation)
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep by paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care - this includes food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement that equals or exceeds the Child Benefit amount

Since you mentioned you live in the UK with Alex, I'm assuming you live together - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You meet the responsibility requirement as Alex lives with you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. For example:
- Is Alex's other parent (if applicable) claiming Child Benefit for them?
- Is anyone else (like a grandparent or other family member) claiming for Alex?

If there is another claimant, we need to know if you've agreed who should claim, or if there's a dispute.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! No one else is claiming for Alex, so there's no conflict.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to ask about Alex's living situation.

**Which of the following describes Alex's current living situation?**

- Is Alex currently in hospital?
- Is Alex in local authority care?
- Is Alex fostered?
- Or is Alex living normally at home with you?

Since you mentioned Alex lives at home with you, I'm assuming it's the normal living situation, but I need to confirm there are no special circumstances.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living normally at home with me. They're not in hospital, care, or fostered - just a regular home situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is living normally at home with you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Almost there! Just one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the council paying for Alex's living costs or upkeep? This would typically only apply in specific circumstances like when a child is in care.

Since Alex is living normally at home with you, I'm expecting the council isn't paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the council isn't paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. We're just a normal family living at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's the final check completed.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
# 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for Alex.

## Why You're Eligible:

✅ **Alex is a qualifying child** - At 13 years old, Alex is under 16  
✅ **Alex is not working full-time** - Not working at all (under 24 hours/week)  
✅ **Alex doesn't receive benefits** - Not receiving ESA or Universal Credit in their own right  
✅ **Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship** - Just attending regular school  
✅ **You meet residency requirements** - You normally live in the UK  
✅ **You're responsible for Alex** - Alex lives at home with you  
✅ **No competing claims** - No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex  
✅ **Normal living situation** - Alex lives at home, not in hospital, care, or fostering  
✅ **Council not paying** - Local council isn't paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance

## Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay some or all of it back through tax
5. **Payments** - You'll receive payments every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

**You can apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Do you have any questions about the application process or anything else?

reasonings: Alex is 13 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with the claimant in the UK, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them, living in a normal home situation, and the local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their child Alex. The claimant normally resides in the UK, is responsible for Alex (living together), and Alex meets all qualifying child criteria.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 13 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with the claimant in the UK, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them, living in a normal home situation, and the local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their child Alex. The claimant normally resides in the UK, is responsible for Alex (living together), and Alex meets all qualifying child criteria.
